metadata.note: Transition line separated from literal hopping; 23.87 GHz conversion corrected to about 9.87e-5 eV; molecular-beam maser mechanism scoped.

skin_warnings: B15: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. In ammonia, nitrogen can sit on either side of the plane made by three hydrogens. Classically those two pyramid shapes are equivalent. Quantum mechanically, tunneling refuses to leave them as two separate stationary states.

props.command: How does ammonia tunneling create a 23.87-gigahertz microwave transition?
props.segment: The Molecule That Tunnels Through Itself
output: Tunneling creates symmetric and antisymmetric levels.

beats.narration_text: Draw the three hydrogens as a plane. Nitrogen above it is one localized geometry; nitrogen below it is the mirror image. Ignoring tiny external asymmetries, the two configurations have the same classical energy.

Use the inversion coordinate as one dimension. The molecular potential becomes a double well: left and right minima separated by a barrier at the planar configuration. A classical particle below the barrier stays in one well.

Quantum wavefunctions leak through the barrier. The left-localized and right-localized basis states overlap, creating a tunneling matrix element A. Even a small overlap changes which combinations are stationary.
The energy eigenstates are the symmetric combination, left plus right, and the antisymmetric combination, left minus right. Tunneling lowers one and raises the other relative to the uncoupled energy.
If the off-diagonal coupling is minus A, the two energies are E zero minus A and E zero plus A. The inversion splitting is therefore delta E equals two A.

A molecule in either energy eigenstate has a stationary probability density. But prepare a left-localized superposition and its probability shifts between the two sides. That oscillation and the spectral transition share the frequency delta E divided by h.

For the familiar ammonia inversion line near 23.87 gigahertz, delta E equals h nu, about nine-point-eight-seven times ten to the minus five electron volts. Tiny energy split; microwave-scale frequency.

So do not picture a stationary molecule's nitrogen as a bead making twenty-four billion classical trips each second. The exact energy states are delocalized across both geometries. The hopping picture applies only to a localized superposition's changing probability.

The 1954 ammonia molecular-beam maser used an inhomogeneous electric field to focus molecules in the upper inversion state and reject the lower-state population. That produced the population inversion needed for gain.

Send the selected molecules through a microwave cavity tuned near the inversion transition. An incident field stimulates upper-to-lower transitions, adding coherent microwave photons to the cavity mode. That is the maser mechanism.

The splitting is exponentially sensitive to barrier height, width, reduced mass, and molecular motion. A crude double-well model can teach the mechanism while missing the measured frequency; spectroscopy requires a realistic potential and rotational-state bookkeeping.

Your turn. Use Planck's constant, four-point-one-three-six times ten to the minus fifteen electron-volt seconds, and nu equals 23.87 gigahertz. Estimate delta E in electron volts, then name the two stationary combinations.

Multiplying gives delta E about nine-point-eight-seven times ten to the minus five electron volts. The stationary states are the symmetric left-plus-right combination and the antisymmetric left-minus-right combination.
Ammonia Inversion: The Molecule That Tunnels Through Itself. Liam, in for Bear.
